content.assessment: As an explicit no-new-policy counterfactual, this proposal makes visible that demographic contraction alone does not engage any of the documented equity mechanisms—SES-skewed access (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02), Roma segregation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06), or within-school sorting (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)—all of which are independent of aggregate cohort size. The proposal's own risk analysis correctly anticipates that urban flagship schools with path-dependent prestige may retain selectivity intensity even as rural provision withers, which is consistent with the documented geographic clustering of track access in urban areas (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08) and could deepen rather than resolve geographic equity gaps.
opportunities: None identified within this lens beyond serving as a comparison baseline; passive contraction offers no equity-improving mechanism of its own
strengths: Provides a clear counterfactual baseline against which active equity interventions can be measured
Avoids introduction of new legally contestable mechanisms (e.g., SES-based admission criteria)
threats: Geographic unevenness of contraction risks worsening rather than improving equity, as urban flagship prestige-driven oversubscription may persist while rural provision disproportionately withers, compounding documented rural-urban access disparities (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)

Church/private schools outside demographic consolidation pressure could gain relative share, and given documented association between privatization growth and segregation amplification, this could worsen rather than improve system-wide sorting (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)

Persistent within-school and primary-choice sorting mechanisms continue entirely unaddressed, meaning aggregate contraction of track share need not translate into reduced inequality of access or outcome (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
weaknesses: Leaves the SES-skewed sorting mechanism fully intact and unaddressed since demographic contraction is orthogonal to who gains access (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Does not engage Roma segregation, a distinct and worsening mechanism unrelated to cohort size (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
Does not address the capital-intensive pre-selection competition documented for disadvantaged families (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
